questions.passageText: All over the world till lately and in most of the world till today, mankind has been following the course of nature: that is to say, it has been breeding up to the maximum. To let nature take her course in the reproduction of the human race may have made sense in an age in which we were also letting her take her course in decimating mankind by the casualties of war, pestilence, and famine. Being human, we have at least revolted against that senseless waste. We have started to impose on nature's heartless play a humane new order of our own. But when once man has begun to interfere with nature, he cannot afford to stop half way. We cannot, with impunity, cut down the death-rate and at the same time, allow the birthrate to go on taking nature's course. We must consciously try to establish an equilibrium, or sooner or later, famine will stalk abroad again.
questions.question: The author observes that
options: war, pestilence and famine were caused by the extravagance of nature
there was a time when uncontrolled birth made sense
It was wise at a time when mankind did not interfere with normal reproduction
questions.explanation: The author states that 'to let nature take her course in the reproduction of the human race may have made sense' in an age when war, pestilence, and famine were also allowed to take their course.
questions.hint: Look for what the author says 'may have made sense' in a past age.

questions.question: Which of these statements does not express the opinion of the author?
options: Mankind has started to interfere with the work of nature
Many people had died in the past through want and disease
Mankind should not have the maximum number of children possible
Mankind should take care of its children
questions.explanation: The passage does not state that 'mankind should take care of its children.' The author discusses birth control and population equilibrium.
questions.hint: Find the statement that is NOT mentioned or implied in the passage.

questions.question: 'Humane', as used in the passage means
questions.explanation: 'Humane' in this context means compassionate, kind, and benevolent - showing sympathy and care for others.
questions.hint: Consider the contrast with 'nature's heartless play'.

questions.question: 'We must consciously try to establish an equilibrium' implies that mankind must
questions.explanation: An 'equilibrium' means a state of balance. The author says mankind must consciously establish a balance between birth rate and death rate.
questions.hint: What does 'equilibrium' mean?

questions.question: The main idea of this passage is that
options: man should control the birth rate
mankind will soon perish of starvation
pestilence causes more death than war
questions.explanation: The central argument is that man must consciously establish an equilibrium by controlling the birth rate.
questions.hint: What is the author's main argument about what mankind should do?

questions.passageText: The endeavor to maintain proper standards of fairness in journalism must be pursued. It is fatally easy for the journalist to deviate from the straight path. There is his natural desire to make a 'story' and the insidious temptation to twist facts to square with his paper's policy. Both are as indefensible as the framing of misleading headlines for the sake of effect. The conscientious journalist must check any tendency to bias, and guard against the dangers inherent in personal antipathies or friendships, and in traditional opposition between rival schools of thought. When a political opponent, whose stupidity habitually provokes attack, makes an effective speech, honesty requires that he be given credit for it. Where personal relationships might make it easier and more congenial to keep silent than to criticize, the journalist must never forget his duty to the public and the supreme importance of recording the truth.
questions.question: When may a journalist have to act against his own inclination?
options: When he becomes weak and decrepit
When his enemies make a mistake
When his political opponents frame misleading headlines
questions.explanation: The passage states that when a political opponent makes an effective speech, honesty requires that he be given credit for it, which may be against the journalist's natural inclination.
questions.hint: Look for when the journalist must give credit to someone he normally opposes.

questions.question: A journalist would be able to uphold the tenets of his profession if
options: he can manipulate his story to please his employers
questions.explanation: The passage states that 'the conscientious journalist must check any tendency to bias' to uphold professional standards.
questions.hint: What does the passage say a conscientious journalist must do?

questions.question: 'Insidious' (line 2) means
questions.explanation: 'Insidious' means proceeding in a gradual, subtle way but with harmful effects. In context, it refers to a temptation that works subtly and dangerously.
questions.hint: Think of a temptation that works slowly and secretly.

questions.question: A dutiful journalist must
options: be able to recognize merit wherever found
questions.explanation: The passage states that honesty requires giving credit to a political opponent who makes an effective speech, meaning recognizing merit even in opponents.
questions.hint: What does the passage say about giving credit to political opponents?

questions.question: The duty of the journalist to the public entails
options: giving credit to his political enemies
questions.explanation: The passage concludes that the journalist 'must never forget his duty to the public and the supreme importance of recording the truth.'
questions.hint: What does the passage say is of 'supreme importance'?

questions.passageText: So far, I have been speaking of science in its universality viewed from the perspective of the world at large. For in the context of our own country and our sister developing countries many of the factors mentioned earlier are not very important. For example, pollution, deterioration of the environment and population explosion are not yet serious problems for us in this country. Let me now turn to a more specific area, namely, the question of scientific choice for developing countries. There is no doubt that the role which science and technology have played in the upliftment of the material and economic well-being of the developed nations will, and does, influence the criteria that the Third World nations must choose in order to establish their science policies and priorities. But the criteria to be used by these nations do not have to be the same as those which have brought the developed countries to their present stage of evolution. For while human beings have the same problems, their solutions, to be meaningful, will have to be sought within some relevant frame of reference such as the available resources and expertise, social values, place and time in the historical scale.
questions.question: 'Frame of reference' in this passage means
questions.explanation: 'Frame of reference' means the context or set of criteria within which solutions must be sought, including available resources, expertise, social values, etc.
questions.hint: Look at what follows: 'such as the available resources and expertise, social values, place and time'.

questions.question: The writer apparently believes that through science and technology our country can achieve
questions.explanation: The passage mentions 'upliftment of the material and economic well-being' as the role science and technology have played in developed nations.
questions.hint: What does the passage say science and technology have done for developed nations?

questions.question: According to the passage, the basic consideration for developing science and technology should be three of the following: 1. technical know-how 2. availability of raw materials 3. atmospheric pollution 4. the people's tradition and beliefs 5. population 6. capital
questions.explanation: The passage mentions 'available resources and expertise' (raw materials/technical know-how), 'social values' (tradition and beliefs), and capital is implied in resources.
questions.hint: Look at what the passage lists as the 'relevant frame of reference'.

questions.question: The term 'our sister developing countries' implies
questions.explanation: The phrase 'sister developing countries' refers to other African developing nations. Liberia, Sierra-Leone, and Gabon are all African developing countries.
questions.hint: Which set of countries are developing African nations?

questions.passageTitle: Altitude and Weather in Mountain Climbing
questions.passageText: These two factors, the altitude and the weather, tend separately and together to defeat the climber. The height weakness slows him down, it forces him to spend days and night in the course of his assault on the summit: the weather, besides adding to the demands of his energy and moral fortitude, conspires to deny him the time he needs to complete his mission. Whereas in lower mountains and on easy ground the weather may be no more than a handicap, in the high Himalayas it is decisive, regardless of terrain. The deduction to be drawn from these two factors was clear enough. We must either so fortify ourselves that we could continue, without detriment, to live and have our being above the limit of natural acclimatization, or better still, we must solve the problem of speed. It was desirable, in fact, that we should meet both these requirements and thus give to those chosen to attempt the summit and to their supporting teams some measure of insurance against the vagaries of the weather, for safety in mountain climbing is as much a matter of swiftness as of sureness of foot. Either or both could be achieved only by the administration of oxygen in sufficient quantities to make up for the deficiency in the air, and for the duration of the upward journey above the limit of successful acclimatization.
questions.question: The author believes that to overcome the problem of altitude and weather, the climber needs mainly
questions.explanation: The passage states that 'Either or both could be achieved only by the administration of oxygen in sufficient quantities.'
questions.hint: What does the passage say is the way to achieve both fortification and speed?

questions.question: 'Conspires' (line 3) means
questions.explanation: 'Conspires' here means 'combines' or 'works together' - the weather combines with other factors to deny the climber time.
questions.hint: Think of weather 'conspiring' with other factors - working together.

questions.question: 'Regardless of terrain' (line 5) means
options: despite the nature of the ground
without considering the type of land
questions.explanation: 'Regardless of terrain' means 'despite the nature of the ground' - the weather is decisive no matter what the ground is like.
questions.hint: 'Regardless' means 'despite' or 'without considering'.

questions.question: 'To live and have our being above the limit of natural acclimatization' (line 7) means
options: to be protected from the effects of the weather
to expect no protection from the bad weather
to survive under unnatural weather conditions
limit our natural desires to get acclimatized to the weather
questions.explanation: The phrase means to be able to survive and function above the altitude where natural acclimatization is possible, i.e., protected by artificial means (oxygen).
questions.hint: What would allow climbers to 'continue without detriment' above natural limits?

questions.question: Which ONE of the following was NOT a reason for using oxygen?
options: it fortified them to live above the limit of natural acclimatization
it protected them against the unexpected changes of the weather
It quickened their journey to the summit
questions.explanation: Psychological encouragement is not mentioned in the passage as a reason for using oxygen. The passage mentions fortification, protection against weather, and increasing speed.
questions.hint: Check each reason against what the passage actually states.

questions.passageText: You dare not lift the veil that protects our tribal marriage customs and ceremonies from the eyes of outsiders. There is already too little in this world that is sacred; and what Fatmata and I were required to go through during the next week must remain so. I was deeply in debt by the time everything was over, but I was absolutely satisfied with my parent's choice. Fatmata was as black as satin, and as soft. She had the teeth and smile of a goddess. The Dopo tutors had done their job well; she was a complete efficient lover and mother. I brought her home with swelling pride, and began at once to save all I could spare towards the cost of acquiring my second wife.
questions.question: The evidence would seem to indicate that
options: the author regrets the cause of his debt
questions.explanation: The author describes Fatmata positively ('as black as satin, as soft', 'teeth and smile of a goddess') and says he was 'absolutely satisfied' with his parents' choice.
questions.hint: Look at the author's positive descriptions of his wife.

questions.question: When the author said 'My anti-Western revolt was gaining momentum' (line 7, not in this excerpt - from original PDF page 5), he was referring to the fact that
options: he did not want to marry another wife
questions.explanation: The 'anti-Western revolt' refers to rejecting European values that condemn polygamy and tribal marriage customs.
questions.hint: What values would Western culture impose that the author is rejecting?

questions.question: It would appear that the marriage had turned him into
questions.explanation: The passage states 'I was deeply in debt by the time everything was over' and he began saving for a second wife, suggesting debt is ongoing.
questions.hint: What financial condition does the author describe?

questions.question: Which one of the following is NOT true of the passage?
options: The marriage cost a lot of money
The marriage was according to native law and custom
questions.explanation: The passage does not mention whether Fatmata had children or not. It says she was a 'complete efficient lover and mother' (capable of being a mother), but not that she never had an issue.
questions.hint: Look for what is stated and what is not stated in the passage.

questions.question: 'Swelling' (line 6) means
questions.explanation: 'Swelling pride' means increasing or growing pride. It suggests the pride is intensifying.
questions.hint: What does 'swelling' mean when referring to an emotion?

questions.question: In the match against the uplanders team, the submariners turned out to be the dark horse
questions.explanation: A 'dark horse' is a previously unknown or little-known competitor who wins unexpectedly.
questions.hint: Think of a horse race where an unknown horse wins.

questions.question: Only the small fry get punished for such social misdemeanors
questions.explanation: 'Small fry' refers to unimportant or insignificant people, as opposed to the 'big fish' who escape punishment.
questions.hint: Who usually gets caught - the important people or the unimportant ones?

questions.question: He spoke with his heart in his mouth
options: with a lot of confusion in his speech
questions.explanation: 'With one's heart in one's mouth' means feeling very nervous, frightened, or anxious.
questions.hint: How does a person feel when they are very scared or nervous?

questions.question: The leader in today's issue of our popular newspaper focuses on inflation
questions.explanation: The 'leader' in a newspaper refers to the leading article or editorial, which expresses the newspaper's opinion.
questions.hint: What is another name for the main opinion article in a newspaper?

questions.question: From the way my friend talks, you can see he is such a bore
questions.explanation: A 'bore' is a person who is dull, uninteresting, and tedious to be around.
questions.hint: What kind of person makes you feel bored?

questions.question: His jail terms were to run concurrently
questions.explanation: 'Concurrently' means at the same time. When jail terms run concurrently, the prisoner serves them at the same time rather than one after another.
questions.hint: What is the opposite of 'consecutively'?

questions.question: There is some obvious symmetry in the whole presentation
questions.explanation: 'Symmetry' means balanced proportions, harmony, and orderliness in arrangement.
questions.hint: Think of something well-balanced and orderly.

questions.question: The bill has to wait as we are now insolvent
questions.explanation: 'Insolvent' means unable to pay debts owed, i.e., bankrupt.
questions.hint: What happens when a person or company cannot pay their debts?

questions.question: All his plans fell through
questions.explanation: 'Fell through' means failed to happen or be completed; came to nothing.
questions.hint: When a plan 'falls through', does it succeed or fail?

questions.question: The balance sheet at the end of the business year shows that we broke even
options: had no money to continue business
questions.explanation: 'Broke even' means that income equals expenditure; no profit and no loss.
questions.hint: What does 'even' suggest - equal on both sides?

questions.question: He was appointed specifically to put the recruits through
questions.explanation: 'Put through' in this context means to train or instruct someone thoroughly.
questions.hint: What do recruits need when they first join?

questions.question: The result of his experiment represents a breakthrough in medical science
questions.explanation: A 'breakthrough' is a significant and important advance or discovery.
questions.hint: Is a breakthrough good or bad news?

questions.question: Being an optimist, our professor always sees the bright side of most things
questions.explanation: The 'bright side' refers to a positive, hopeful, or cheerful perspective.
questions.hint: What kind of person always sees the positive?

questions.question: The state government appointed a commission of inquiry to go into the community's complaints carefully and without prejudice
questions.explanation: 'Go into' in this context means to investigate or examine thoroughly.
questions.hint: What does a commission of inquiry do?

questions.question: The traffic situation in Lagos can lead to disastrous consequences; a man lost a very lucrative job because he was held up by it
questions.explanation: 'Held up' means delayed or obstructed.
questions.hint: What happens when traffic causes you to be late?

questions.question: Mrs. Dada has been deserted by her husband because he feels she has a heart of stone
options: she has little warmth of feeling
questions.explanation: A 'heart of stone' means being cold, unfeeling, and lacking compassion. 'Hard-hearted' has the same meaning.
questions.hint: Is a stone warm or cold?

questions.question: Mr. Jack could be a successful businessman if he paid more attention to the more intricate aspects of his account.
options: Jack will have a very good chance of succeeding
Mr. Jack will find it difficult to succeed
questions.explanation: The sentence implies that currently Mr. Jack does NOT pay enough attention to intricate aspects, so he will find it difficult to succeed.
questions.hint: Is he currently paying attention or not?

questions.question: If my father had not arrived, I would have starved. This sentence means
options: My father did arrive and I didn't starve
I had to starve because my father didn't come
my father didn't arrive and I didn't starve
I should have starved but I didn't
questions.explanation: This is a third conditional sentence. It means that the father DID arrive, so the speaker did NOT starve.
questions.hint: What does 'if...had not...would have' imply about what actually happened?

questions.question: The two sprinters were running neck and neck
questions.explanation: 'Neck and neck' means exactly level or tied in a race or competition.
questions.hint: What does it mean when two runners are side by side?

questions.question: He stared at her
questions.explanation: To 'stare' means to look fixedly and intently, often for a long time. 'Gazed' has a similar meaning of looking steadily.
questions.hint: Which word suggests looking steadily for a long time?

questions.question: When the man was caught by police, he presented a bold front
options: he walked up to the policemen
he faced the situation with apparent boldness
he bravely attempted to give them a present
questions.explanation: To 'present a bold front' means to appear brave and confident even if one is not feeling that way inside.
questions.hint: What does 'front' suggest - appearance or reality?

questions.question: I have been able to observe him at close quarters
options: within a short space of time
questions.explanation: 'At close quarters' means from a close distance or in close proximity.
questions.hint: What does 'quarters' mean in terms of distance?

questions.question: At the site of the ghastly accident the poor woman's hair stood on end
options: the wind blew her hair upwards
questions.explanation: 'Hair stood on end' is an expression meaning to be extremely frightened or horrified.
questions.hint: What emotion makes your hair stand on end?

questions.question: When the beggar was tired, he .... down by the roadside
questions.explanation: 'Lay' is the past tense of 'lie' (to recline). 'Lied' is past tense of 'lie' (to tell untruth). 'Laid' is past tense of 'lay' (to put down).
questions.hint: Does he recline or put something down?

questions.question: He did not like .... leaving the class early
questions.explanation: Before a gerund (-ing form used as a noun), we use a possessive adjective (our) rather than an object pronoun (us).
questions.hint: Do we say 'me leaving' or 'my leaving'?

questions.question: Before the operation, the dentist found that his patient's teeth....
questions.explanation: The past perfect 'had decayed' is used because the decay happened before the dentist's finding (past action).
questions.hint: Which tense shows an action completed before another past action?

questions.question: The employer, not the salesman and his representative .... responsible for the loss
questions.explanation: The subject is 'the employer' (singular), not the plural phrase that follows. The verb must be singular 'is'.
questions.hint: Who is the main subject of the sentence?

questions.question: The boy was born before his parents actually got married and so the court has declared him ....
questions.explanation: 'Illegitimate' is the correct term for a child born to parents who are not legally married to each other.
questions.hint: What is the legal term for a child born out of wedlock?

questions.question: As he was still owing me two naira, I was careful not to .... him any more money
questions.explanation: 'Lend' means to give something temporarily. 'Borrow' means to receive something temporarily. The subject is giving money, so 'lend' is correct.
questions.hint: Is he giving or receiving money?

questions.question: Last week the .... at the theater was the longest I had ever seen
questions.explanation: 'Audience' is the correct collective noun for people gathered to watch a performance at a theater.
questions.hint: What do you call people watching a play?

questions.question: If you travel by air you will be given an allowance to cover ....
questions.explanation: 'Luggage' is an uncountable noun and does not take an article 'a' or a plural form.
questions.hint: Is 'luggage' countable or uncountable?

questions.question: Tennyson and Browning lived about the same time and therefore .... poets
questions.explanation: 'Contemporary' means living or occurring at the same time. 'Contemporaneous' is also correct but less common; the answer key shows D (contemporary).
questions.hint: What do you call people who live at the same time?

questions.question: The green snake which lives in the green grass is using a perfect ....
questions.explanation: 'Camouflage' is the use of coloring or patterns to blend in with the environment for concealment.
questions.hint: What allows an animal to blend in with its surroundings?

questions.question: In order to carry out necessary examination the dead body was ....
questions.explanation: 'Exhumed' means to dig up a dead body from the ground for examination.
questions.hint: What is the term for digging up a buried body?

questions.question: Remember this matter is strictly ....
questions.explanation: After the preposition 'between', we use object pronouns: 'me' not 'I'. 'Between you and me' is correct.
questions.hint: What pronoun comes after a preposition?

questions.question: He went abroad with a view .... a business partner
questions.explanation: The phrase is 'with a view to' followed by a gerund (-ing form). 'To finding' is correct.
questions.hint: What form of verb follows 'to' when it is part of the phrase 'with a view to'?

questions.question: He had just entered the office .... the telephone rang
questions.explanation: 'When' is used to indicate that one action happened immediately after another, often with 'had just' in the first clause.
questions.hint: Which word shows that the telephone rang at that moment?

questions.question: They .... receive visitors on short notice
questions.topic: Used to vs. Be used to
questions.explanation: 'Used to' (without 'be') indicates a past habit or state that is no longer true. The answer key shows C (used to).
questions.hint: Does this describe a past habit or a present one?

questions.question: His suggestions are completely .... the point and cannot be accepted
questions.explanation: 'Beside the point' is an idiom meaning irrelevant or not related to the matter being discussed.
questions.hint: What phrase means 'irrelevant'?

questions.question: For .... he is a secretary we shall not have correct minutes
questions.explanation: 'As long as' means 'since' or 'because' in this context, indicating a condition or reason.
questions.hint: Which phrase means 'since' or 'considering that'?

questions.question: He went up quickly and returned ....
questions.explanation: The correct comparative structure is 'as fast as' or simply 'as fast' in this elliptical construction.
questions.hint: What is the correct adverb form of 'fast'?

questions.question: Invariably, he ends his letters .... amicably
questions.explanation: 'Yours' is the correct possessive pronoun (no apostrophe needed). It means 'your letter'.
questions.hint: What is the possessive pronoun for 'you' without an apostrophe?

questions.question: If I went to the cinema I .... early
questions.explanation: This is a second conditional sentence (unreal present). The structure is 'If + past simple, would + base verb'.
questions.hint: What tense follows 'would' in a conditional sentence?

questions.question: The traditional folktales form an .... to the book
questions.explanation: An 'appendix' is supplementary material added at the end of a book.
questions.hint: What is the section at the end of a book that contains additional information?

questions.question: The frightening explosion in the factory .... the whole wing
questions.explanation: 'Wiped out' means completely destroyed or eliminated.
questions.hint: Which phrasal verb means 'completely destroy'?

questions.question: Our plane was scheduled to .... at 10.00 hrs. but it was delayed because of the bad weather
questions.explanation: 'Take off' is the specific phrasal verb used for an airplane leaving the ground.
questions.hint: What do planes do when they begin their flight?

questions.question: As luck would have it, the weather brightened almost immediately and after six hours flight we .... at Heathrow Airport
questions.explanation: 'Touched down' is the phrasal verb meaning to land (of an aircraft).
questions.hint: What do planes do when they land?

questions.question: Before we left the airport, each passenger was made to .... certain forms
questions.explanation: 'Fill in' means to complete a form by writing in the required information.
questions.hint: What do you do with a form?

questions.question: My father cannot bear the .... of the concoction that emanates from my mother's soup pot
questions.explanation: 'Smell' is the most appropriate word here, as it refers to an unpleasant odor detected through the nose.
questions.hint: What word describes an odor, especially an unpleasant one?

questions.question: I would not have condescended to appease the traffic policeman but I happened .... traffic regulation
questions.explanation: 'Happened to have broken' uses the perfect infinitive to indicate that the breaking occurred before the appeasing.
questions.hint: Which form shows that the breaking happened earlier?

questions.question: The managing director did not pay his staff last month ...?
questions.explanation: For a negative statement, the tag is positive. 'Did not pay' is negative, so the tag is 'did he'.
questions.hint: What is the rule for tag questions with negative statements?

questions.question: Although he is in all respects a poor student, he has managed to buy a tape recorder to improve his .... of French
questions.explanation: 'Mastery' means comprehensive knowledge or skill in a subject.
questions.hint: What word means 'complete command or knowledge' of something?

questions.question: Granted you are my senior by one year, you cannot .... me around as if I were your servant
questions.explanation: 'Order' means to command or tell someone what to do in a domineering way.
questions.hint: What verb means to give commands in an authoritative way?

questions.question: .... mark an important step in the history of industrial revolution
options: The invention of the locomotive engine
questions.explanation: The invention of the locomotive engine was a key development in the Industrial Revolution.
questions.hint: What invention revolutionized land transportation?

questions.question: Because the referee was partial and his opponent rough, the loser of the wrestling match received ... cheers than the winner
questions.explanation: 'Many more' is the correct comparative phrase for countable nouns like 'cheers'.
questions.hint: Is 'cheers' countable or uncountable?

questions.question: The boxer was highly rated .... his doggedness and ability to absorb heavy punches
questions.explanation: 'For' is used to indicate the reason or basis for the rating.
questions.hint: Which preposition shows the reason for something?

questions.question: Many lives are lost on Nigerian roads .... lack of consideration for other road users by many crazy drivers
questions.explanation: 'Through' can mean 'because of' or 'as a result of' and fits naturally in this context.
questions.hint: Which preposition can mean 'as a result of'?

questions.question: The man no longer feared his opponent after their spokesman had assured him that they were well disposed .... him
questions.explanation: The correct phrase is 'well disposed toward' meaning having a favorable attitude.
questions.hint: What preposition follows 'disposed' when meaning attitude?

questions.question: My little boy is hyper-active and he is therefore prone .... accidents
questions.explanation: 'Prone to' is the correct collocation meaning likely to suffer from or be affected by.
questions.hint: What preposition follows 'prone'?

questions.question: His horrible high-pitched laugh .... on my nerves
questions.explanation: 'Jars on my nerves' is an idiom meaning irritates or annoys me greatly.
questions.hint: Which verb means to have an irritating effect?

questions.question: The train rattled along .... full speed
questions.explanation: 'At full speed' is the correct prepositional phrase meaning traveling as fast as possible.
questions.hint: What preposition is used with 'speed'?

questions.question: You would have thought that somebody would have informed me ....
questions.explanation: The tag relates to 'you would have thought', so the tag is 'wouldn't you'.
questions.hint: What is the tag for 'You would have thought'?

questions.question: If this had happened to you, what ....?
questions.explanation: This is a third conditional sentence (past unreal). The structure is 'If + past perfect, would have + past participle'.
questions.hint: What tense follows 'would' in a past unreal conditional?

questions.question: What a relief that the strike .... when it did!
questions.explanation: The simple past 'ended' is used because the strike ended at a specific time in the past.
questions.hint: Which tense describes a completed past action?

questions.question: We should take care .... the robbers come back
questions.explanation: 'In case' is used to express precaution against a possible future event.
questions.hint: Which phrase means 'as a precaution against the possibility that'?

questions.question: By the time the rainy season ended, all the young cocoa trees .... in size
questions.explanation: The past perfect 'had doubled' is used because the doubling happened before the rainy season ended.
questions.hint: Which tense shows an action completed before another past action?

questions.question: There were so many children ...
options: that she couldn't feed them all
that she could feed them all
questions.explanation: The structure is 'so many... that' to express result. The clause must include 'them all' for completeness.
questions.hint: What is the correct structure for expressing result with 'so many'?

questions.question: Isn't it high time you .... your office?
questions.explanation: After 'it's high time', we use the past subjunctive (past tense) to express that something should be done now.
questions.hint: What tense follows 'it's high time'?

questions.question: In West Africa the .... of sickle cell is about 25 percent
questions.explanation: 'Incidence' means the rate or frequency of occurrence of a disease. 'Incident' means an event.
questions.hint: What word means 'rate of occurrence'?

questions.question: We got to the hall after the play ....
questions.explanation: The past perfect 'had started' is used because the play started before we arrived.
questions.hint: Which tense shows an action completed before another past action?
